channel.title: Tutorial on Bazlur's Stream of Thoughts
channel.description: Recent content in Tutorial on Bazlur's Stream of Thoughts

item.title: How to Handle Checked Exceptions With Lambda Expression
item.description: Lambda expressions are all about how we write code. It’s about how we can write more concise, smaller, and less boilerplate code. However, this aforementioned statement may not seem to be true in the case of exceptions in Java. In Java, we can only handle exceptions through the try-catch block, and this hasn’t changed for the lambda expression. Let’s say we’re going to develop a simple web crawler. The crawler will take a list of URLs in a string as an argument and save the content of the text file in a text file.

item.title: We Don't Need StringBuilder for Simple Concatenation
item.description: String concatenation has always been a well-discussed topic among Java developers. It’s been costly. Let’s first dig into the issue of why it’s costly. In Java, string objects are immutable, which means once it is created, you cannot change it. So when we concatenate one string with another, a new string is created, and the older one is marked for the garbage collector. Let’s say we need to concatenate a million strings.
